episodes.title: AI Spending: A New Engine for the Global Economy
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Morgan Stanley's quarterly economist roundtable (part 1 of 2) frames the AI-driven capex cycle as the dominant force in the global economy, tracing how US hyperscaler spending is fueling growth abroad — particularly in Asia — more than domestically, while Europe lags far behind and wrestles with a structural fiscal squeeze.

**Key points**
- US hyperscaler/AI-related capex estimates revised up to $1.2-1.4tn by 2027-28 (~3.5% of GDP), but ~60% of that spend is high import-content equipment, so it's adding only ~40bp to US GDP growth this year and next.
- Asia semiconductor exports to the US are growing ~90%, tightly correlated with US IT capex; Korea, Taiwan and Japan are the biggest beneficiaries.
- Morgan Stanley sees a broader Asian "capex super-cycle": AI/semis ($380bn in 2026), energy ($900bn — far larger than AI), defense, and industrial reshoring; expected to run 3-4 more years given healthy corporate balance sheets (corporate debt/GDP below 2019 levels).
- US household net worth has risen $55tn in five years, partly AI-driven, supporting upper-income consumer spending — growth is narrowly concentrated in AI-linked investment and wealth effects.
- Europe's AI investment plans are roughly 1/20th the size of the top US hyperscalers'; fiscal policy faces structural headwinds from aging costs, rising defense spending, and higher debt-servicing costs, with growth (currently ~1% potential) seen as the only real solution; Germany's fiscal package has yet to spur meaningful private investment.

**FX / rates implications**
- Sustained semiconductor export strength is a structural positive for KRW and TWD, and supportive for JPY-linked tech exporters; a broadening capex cycle (energy, defense, reshoring) extends the growth tailwind for North Asia beyond the AI narrative alone.
- US growth resilience being import-heavy and wealth-effect driven (not broad-based) argues against outsized USD strength purely from domestic demand; equity-driven wealth effects tie US consumption resilience to risk sentiment/S&P 500 performance.
- EUR faces a structural growth and fiscal disadvantage versus US/Asia, reinforcing the case for EUR underperformance until German/French private investment materially recovers.

**Watch list**
- Part 2 of the roundtable (following day) will cover inflation and policy implications of this growth outlook — relevant for Fed/ECB rate paths.
- Signs of German/French private investment pickup as a Europe growth catalyst.
- Any deceleration in US-bound Asian semiconductor export growth as a signal the capex super-cycle is peaking.

episodes.title: Big Tech on Deck, Congress’ Midterm To-Do List, and Cyclospora Outbreak 7/20/26
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
US markets rebounded on an AI-infrastructure bid even as the S&P has not made a new closing high since June, while a ninth consecutive night of US strikes on Iran, a new Houthi maritime embargo threat against Saudi Arabia, and $4+ national gasoline prices keep an oil-supply risk premium alive. None of the content is EM-specific, but the oil/geopolitics and risk-sentiment threads carry direct transmission into EM currencies and rates.

**Key points**
- Iran conflict: ninth straight night of US strikes; two US soldiers confirmed killed in strikes on Al Tanf/Jordan base; Iran suspended MOU commitments but signaled talks could resume; Trump vowed retaliation for soldier deaths.
- Houthis declared an immediate maritime embargo on Saudi Arabia, threatening the Bab al-Mandeb strait as an alternate oil route alongside the Strait of Hormuz (traffic there already at a three-week low).
- Oil prices off session highs on Iran talk-resumption comments; gasoline back above $4/gallon nationally.
- Chinese open-weight AI models (Moonshot's Kimi K3, Alibaba's Qwen) triggered a tech/semiconductor selloff last week (Philly Semiconductor Index down 10% on the week, in bear-market territory, -20% from June 22 peak); guests (Doug Clinton, Darrell Cronk of Wells Fargo Investment Institute) call it positioning/momentum unwind, not a fundamental AI-demand hit, and expect a bounce near 50/200-day moving averages.
- Congress has fewer than 20 legislative days before midterms, with a September 30 government-shutdown deadline, a Pentagon reconciliation funding fight (~$60bn provision), Clarity Act, and a possible Russia sanctions bill in play — Pentagon Iran-strike funding from the prior $150bn appropriation reportedly running low.
- Paramount-Warner Bros Discovery merger hit with a US court temporary restraining order (up to 14 days) on antitrust grounds; costly for Paramount given a ticking fee past September 30 deadline.

**FX / rates implications**
- Escalating Iran conflict and new Red Sea/Hormuz-adjacent chokepoint threats (Houthi embargo) are a bullish oil-supply-risk catalyst — negative for EM oil importers (INR, PHP, IDR, TRY-type sensitivity) via terms-of-trade and current-account pressure if sustained; watch Brent/WTI reaction if embargo becomes enforceable.
- Broad risk-off in tech/semis (SOX bear market) is a risk-sentiment headwind that can pressure high-beta EM Asia FX (KRW, TWD, MYR) given their tech-export linkages, though guests view this as consolidation, not a fundamental turn — a stabilization here would support carry trades broadly.
- No direct Fed/DXY commentary in this episode, limiting the immediate USD read-through, but sustained oil strength plus fiscal deadline uncertainty (shutdown risk end-September) are latent UST/dollar catalysts.

**Watch list**
- Whether the Houthi maritime embargo on Saudi Arabia becomes operationally enforceable — a second chokepoint disruption alongside Hormuz.
- Iran-US diplomatic signals following Iran's talk-resumption comments and Rubio's openness to diplomacy.
- US government funding/shutdown deadline September 30 and Pentagon reconciliation funding vote.
- This week's hyperscaler earnings (Alphabet, Intel, ServiceNow) for capex guidance and semiconductor demand signals.

episodes.title: Cut to the Chase! ‘Non-exuberance’ in the summer months – Part 3
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Standard Chartered's daily flags that US equity positioning has been unwinding since end-May, dropping from +2 standard deviations to around +0.4/+0.5, but argues this is still too high relative to prior unwind cycles, implying more downside risk ahead for US equities. The episode ties this to a broader "non-exuberance" rotation theme, with Korean and Taiwan equities showing wobbles while the Hang Seng Index holds up as a fund-rotation beneficiary.

**Key points**
- US equity positioning fell from +2 SD peak to roughly +0.4/+0.5 SD since end-May, still elevated versus history.
- In 2-3 prior unwind episodes, positioning fell below zero before the S&P 500 bottomed, suggesting current levels are not yet capitulation.
- Systematic/model-driven flow signals show an absence of positive near-term return bias for the S&P 500; model-driven investors are skewed to the downside.
- The US economic surprise index (actual vs expected data) appears to have peaked, removing a tailwind for equity bulls.
- Seasonality is unfavorable: July entries into US equities rarely produce material gains on a 3-month forward basis historically.
- Korean and Taiwan equities showed weakness ('wobbles') while the Hang Seng Index outperformed, consistent with a rotation into value/defensive plays.

**FX / rates implications**
- A continued unwind in US equity positioning and risk-off skew is a broad negative-risk-sentiment signal that can pressure high-beta EM Asia currencies (KRW, TWD) while supporting rotation-beneficiary markets like Hong Kong/China equities.
- Recommended rotation into defensive US sectors (healthcare, financials) and global value plays (Hang Seng) suggests reduced risk appetite, a mild negative for EM carry trades broadly until positioning resets further.

**Watch list**
- Further deterioration in US equity positioning (below zero historically marks prior bottoms) as a signal for risk sentiment turning.
- Continued relative performance of Korean/Taiwan equities versus Hang Seng Index as a rotation gauge.
- US economic surprise index trend for signs of renewed acceleration or further peaking.

episodes.title: The Week That Was - 20 Jul: A firm week for Indian markets
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Standard Chartered's weekly India wrap covers a firm week for headline Indian equities despite hotter-than-expected inflation prints, a widening trade deficit, rupee weakness past the 96 handle, and rising bond yields, framed against a softer US inflation backdrop that reinforces expectations the Fed's hawkish peak is behind it.

**Key points**
- June US CPI and PPI both surprised to the downside, with easing retail sales giving the Fed more flexibility; base case is rates on hold through H2 2026, though steeper declines in short- vs long-term yields point to persistent fiscal-driven term premium.
- India's June CPI accelerated to 4.38% from 3.93% in May, breaching RBI's 4% medium-term target on elevated food and fuel prices; June WPI rose to 9.87% from 9.68%.
- India's merchandise trade deficit widened sharply to $30.43bn in June (vs $19.10bn a year earlier), with imports up nearly 31% to $70.84bn outpacing 15.5% export growth.
- Net direct tax collections rose 16.4% y/y (corporate tax +22%, securities transaction tax +48%), signaling resilient corporate profitability and capital-market activity.
- Nifty rose 0.53% to 24,334 and Sensex gained 0.75%; IT led sectors (+4.34%) on Fed-easing hopes and strong Q1 FY27 tech earnings, while Auto (+0.89%) and Banks (+0.8%) also gained; mid-caps (-0.97%) and small-caps (-0.62%) lagged, and Realty was weakest (-2.12%) on higher rate expectations.

**FX / rates implications**
- USDINR: rupee weakened about 1% to close near 96.28, extending its slide as oil-price spikes (on US-Iran tensions and Strait of Hormuz risk) and broader dollar strength weighed on the currency.
- India rates: 10-year benchmark yield rose ~6.6bps to 6.78%, a third straight weekly rise, as the CPI beat lifted rate expectations and made borrowing costs stickier — bearish for INR duration near-term.
- Global read-through: softer US CPI/PPI supports the view that Fed hawkishness has peaked, generally USD-negative/EM-supportive over time, but near-term oil firmness (Brent-WTI range seen testing 70-90 range) and dollar strength are working against INR and other EM Asia currencies.
- Gold: higher rates and a firmer dollar may slow near-term gains, but StanChart keeps a 12-month target of $5,100/oz on central bank buying and reserve diversification — relevant as an EM reserve-manager theme.

**Watch list**
- RBI policy response to CPI breaching the 4% medium-term target and its effect on rate-cut timing.
- Oil price trajectory versus the $70-90/bbl range amid Strait of Hormuz risk.
- India trade deficit trend given import growth (31%) sharply outpacing exports (15.5%).
- Next moves in USDINR after breaching the 96 level and in the 10-year yield after a third consecutive weekly rise.

episodes.title: Tale of Two Cities
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
This episode contrasts housing markets in Syracuse, NY and Austin, TX following major semiconductor plant announcements (Micron's $100bn memory chip plant near Syracuse, Samsung's earlier plant near Austin), examining why supply-side regulatory differences cause home prices to diverge sharply after similar demand shocks. It is a domestic US regional economics story with no EM FX/rates or global macro content.

**Key points**
- Micron announced up to $100bn investment near Syracuse in 2022, projected to create 50,000 jobs over a decade; Syracuse home prices already up ~30% over three years, with a local agent forecasting prices could double in 4-5 years absent a construction surge.
- Samsung's 2021 semiconductor plant announcement near Austin coincided with falling home prices there, driven by a construction boom (large national homebuilders building on spec) and an unwind of pandemic-era in-migration.
- Austin's building-friendly permitting/zoning and Texas Sunbelt-style light regulation enabled rapid supply response; Anadaga County (Syracuse) is 74% zoned single-family, with costly, slow multifamily permitting, environmental and wage regulations limiting new supply.
- New York State has passed measures to streamline multifamily permitting and created a low-interest loan fund for builders to try to close the gap.

**FX / rates implications**
None directly — this is a US regional housing/microeconomics piece with no bearing on USD, Fed policy, EM currencies or rates.

**Watch list**
- Whether Syracuse-area rezoning and streamlined permitting materialize quickly enough to expand housing supply before the bulk of Micron's 50,000 jobs arrive over the next decade.
- Pace of home price appreciation in Syracuse (currently ~30% over 3 years) as a bellwether for other US regions receiving large CHIPS Act-style manufacturing investment.

episodes.title: Matthew Smith — Natural Gas: The Next Bottleneck - [Invest Like the Best, EP.483]
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
Energy investor Matthew Smith (Chrono Meter Partners) argues the US is heading into a structural natural gas supply deficit starting 2028, driven by AI data-center demand colliding with rising LNG export commitments (15 to 35 BCF/day by 2030) against a max deliverable production ceiling of ~130-132 BCF/day. He sees working gas storage breached to unprecedented lows by 2029-2030, calling upside price risk "unbounded and convex" — a global commodity supply story with knock-on effects for allies dependent on US LNG.

**Key points**
- Natural gas is over 40% of US power generation and overtaking petroleum as the most important domestic fuel.
- US LNG export capacity rises from ~15 BCF/day today to 35 BCF/day by 2030 on already-financed, hard-to-halt projects; the US will be roughly a third of global gas supply.
- Base case AI compute demand adds ~5 BCF/day; extreme case (fuel cells, distributed gen) could hit 12-15 BCF/day by early 2030s.
- Gas curve is currently complacent: spot ~$3.50-3.60, 2028 futures ~$6.27, flat into 2029-2030 — he expects a sharp repricing, potentially $8-10+/MCF, as utilities start hedging 2028 supply.
- Processing, gathering and interstate pipeline buildout (only one new interstate pipe in 12 years) are binding constraints beyond raw resource in the ground.
- Winners: Expand Energy (trading 4x EBITDA), Range Resources, utility-scale solar (XIFR, Clearway), residential solar, large-scale nuclear (Westinghouse AP1000 via Cameco, BWXT); losers: US consumers, fuel-cell/distributed gen-set makers (Bloom, Caterpillar), gas turbine E&C firms.

**FX / rates implications**
- Direct transmission channel is global gas/commodity prices, not EM FX per se: a US LNG supply squeeze or curtailment of contracted cargoes would tighten global gas balances, pressuring Asian LNG importers (Japan, Korea, and by extension regional energy-import costs relevant to KRW, THB, PHP, INR current-account/inflation dynamics) and forcing Europe toward costlier gas or renewed Russian dependence (EUR/GBP energy-cost sensitivity).
- If realized, higher US electricity/gas costs feed into US inflation prints, complicating Fed easing expectations and indirectly pressuring DXY/UST yields — a slow-burn 2028+ risk rather than a near-term trade.
- Commodity desks should treat US natural gas as an emerging convexity story analogous to prior LNG-driven Europe/Asia gas spikes (Russia-Ukraine, 2022 polar vortex).

**Watch list**
- 2028 gas futures curve repricing as utilities begin contracting/hedging physical supply.
- US working gas storage draws relative to historical ranges starting mid-2028.
- Progress on new interstate pipeline permitting and large-scale nuclear (AP1000) commitments (2033-2034 targeted online dates).
- Any move to curtail contracted (not just spot) LNG cargoes, which would directly hit Japan/Korea utility counterparties and Europe.

episodes.title: The Credit Market Lens: Rich Spreads, Cheap Treasuries, and an Incomplete Explanation
episodes.summary:
**Summary**
PIMCO's Lotfy Karui examines whether unusually tight US corporate credit spreads are an artifact of cheap Treasury valuations rather than genuine credit richness. The episode is a fixed-income relative-value piece, most relevant to a macro trader as a read on how much of today's tight credit spreads reflect Treasury/swap technicals versus real fundamental strength.

**Key points**
- The thesis tested: if Treasuries are cheap relative to interest rate swaps, spreads-to-Treasuries mechanically look tighter than spreads-to-swaps would suggest.
- A scatter plot of monthly changes in swap spreads versus corporate spreads-to-Treasuries shows almost no relationship in the US, undercutting the mechanical explanation for spread moves (though a structural/level effect from negative swap spreads is acknowledged).
- US swap spreads are driven largely by Treasury supply, dealer balance sheet constraints, regulatory capital costs, and hedging flows — factors largely orthogonal to corporate credit risk.
- Europe differs: EUR credit spreads-to-Bunds show a much stronger co-movement with swap spreads, reflecting a more swap-centric market structure and shared exposure to bank funding conditions, amplified by financials' large weight in the Bloomberg Euro Aggregate IG index.
- Day-to-day, US corporate spread changes remain driven mainly by fundamentals, technicals, and the economic cycle rather than Treasury-swap mechanics.

**FX / rates implications**
- Confirms current tight USD IG/HY spreads are not simply a Treasury-cheapness illusion — genuine credit richness persists, consistent with resilient risk sentiment and carry-friendly conditions supportive of EM credit and FX.
- Treasury supply and dealer balance sheet constraints affecting US swap spreads are a structural theme relevant to UST 10y term premium and repo/funding dynamics that indirectly shape global USD funding costs relevant to EM issuers.
- EUR credit's greater swap sensitivity flags bank funding stress as a faster transmission channel into European risk pricing than in the US.

**Watch list**
- Ongoing Treasury issuance/supply trends and their effect on swap spreads and repo/funding conditions.
- Divergence between US and EUR credit-swap co-movement as a signal of relative banking-sector funding stress.
